subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Research question clearly stated and consistently integrated throughout the report
Topic communicated accurately and coherently with well-structured narrative
Methodology choices are justified and appropriate to the research focus
weaknesses: Empirical component limited to a single step size and evaluation point, constraining methodological depth
Occasional overlong proofs and detailed diagrams slightly disrupt narrative flow
Some assumptions (e.g., zero initial current) are not explicitly stated at introduction of the ODE
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two of the three strands (topic communication and research question clarity) fulfil the top band (6 marks), while the methodology strand, though coherent and justified, is slightly constrained by its narrow empirical scope (band 5). On balance this yields a Focus and Method score of 5, reflecting strong focus and communication but room to deepen methodological rigour.

strengths: Excellent application of higher-level mathematics (Laplace transforms, error analysis) demonstrating deep subject knowledge
Accurate and (almost) consistent use of subject-specific terminology and notation
Logical progression from general differential equation to concrete numerical instance
weaknesses: Minor typographical slips in mathematical notation (e.g., duplicate indices)
Incomplete citation details for one source (Tiliksew et al.)
Occasional bracket or subscript errors that do not impede overall comprehension
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates excellent subject knowledge and effectively applies advanced concepts (band 6) but incurs minor lapses in notation consistency and citation completeness (band 5). A composite score of 5 recognises strong knowledge application and terminology use with small areas for polish.

strengths: Well-structured, multi-stage analysis linking derivations, numerical results and theoretical error bounds
Integration of a range of reputable sources to support modelling choices
Thoughtful discussion contrasting theoretical error bounds with empirical findings
weaknesses: Evaluation remains partly descriptive; deeper quantitative comparisons (e.g., multiple step sizes or higher-order methods) could strengthen critical evaluation
Research could draw more extensively on specialist numerical-analysis literature
Some conclusions not fully supported by varied empirical data
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strands show a strong performance: research quality and analysis depth sit solidly in the 7â9 band, while discussion and evaluation fall in the 4â6 band. The overall balance of clear reasoning and some limits in evaluation warrants a Critical Thinking score of 7.

strengths: Clear, IB-appropriate structure with all required elements (abstract, headings, appendices)
Consistent and professional formatting: pagination, word count, equation and figure numbering
Effective use of headings and layout to guide the reader
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both presentation strands meet the highest standards (band 3â4), with logical structure and clean formatting throughout. The Presentation score is therefore the maximum of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research questionââTo what extent can Eulerâs method accurately approximate the solution to the differential equation of the series RL circuit?ââis explicitly stated on the title page and is referred to consistently in the introduction, methodology, analysis and conclusion.  Its scope (single RL circuit, specified component values, single chosen time-step) is narrow enough to permit deep exploration while remaining clearly mathematical.  Each section of the paper is explicitly linked back to answering this question (e.g., need for an analytic solution, derivation of error bounds, comparison of approximation and exact value), demonstrating full focus and integration.  Hence the strand earns the full 6 marks within the 5â6 band.

The student offers **clear, multi-stage analysis**: derivation of the DE, exact solution, numerical approximation, and theoretical/empirical error measures.  Steps are logically sequenced and mostly justified.  Conclusions (e.g., first-order accuracy, effect of step size, influence of Lipschitz constant) are supported by derivations and calculated data.  Nevertheless, empirical depth is capped by considering only one step size and one evaluation point, so quantitative validation of LTE/GTE orders is limited.  This situates the work at the lower end of the 7â9 band; score 7.

Technical vocabularyâLaplace transform, integrating factor, Lipschitz constant, truncation error, Taylor remainder, etc.âis employed accurately and almost always consistently.  Mathematical notation is largely impeccable, with only occasional minor bracket/ sub-script slips (e.g., duplicate index in the LTE table) that do not impede comprehension.  Proofs are set out with correct symbolic conventions and logical sequencing.  Because of the few typographical mishaps, the strand sits solidly in the top band but merits 5 rather than 6.

Formatting is clean and largely professional: consistent font, clear equation numbering, appropriate figure captions, correct citation style, title page, pagination and word count provided.  A few oversized images and sporadic typographical slips (e.g., duplicate step number â2â in Appendix D table header) mildly disrupt polish but do not compromise readability.  Overall layout meets IB expectations, so the strand secures 4 marks in the highest band.

The essay demonstrates **excellent mastery of higher-level mathematics**: Laplace transforms, integration by parts, Taylor-series remainder, Lipschitz conditions and error analysis are applied accurately and in context.  Each concept is carefully introduced, proved where necessary, and then used to answer the research question.  The analytic solution, derivation of LTE/GTE orders, and discussion of stability show deep understanding and correct application.  Occasional minor slips (e.g., small algebraic typos) are acknowledged but do not detract from the overall quality or correctness of the mathematics presented.  The work therefore fulfils the expectations of the top band; score 6.

The student opens with a clear, engaging introduction that outlines the electrical-engineering context, explains why the RL circuit model is interesting, and explicitly states the research focus on âthe extent to which Eulerâs method can accurately approximateâ¦â.  Throughout the essay the topic is revisited logically (e.g., derivation of the DE, analytic solution, numerical approximation, error discussion), creating a seamless narrative that readers can easily follow.  Definitions, figures and sub-headings are used effectively to clarify complex ideas such as KVL, Laplace transforms and error theory.  Minor discursions (e.g., historical anecdotes) are brief and do not impede clarity.  Overall the topic is communicated accurately, coherently and with an unmistakable purpose, justifying placement in the top band and a score of 6.

The essay follows a **logical, IB-appropriate structure**: abstract, contents, introduction, theory, methodology, analysis, conclusion, appendices, references.  Headings, sub-headings and numbered equations/tables/figures guide the reader effectively, and mathematical proofs are placed logically.  Minor instances of lengthy interludes could be streamlined but do not hinder comprehension.  All required elements are present, so the top band (3â4) is merited; full 4 marks are awarded.

A wide range of reputable sources (university texts, MathWorld, journal article on Picard-LindelÃ¶f) are integrated to construct both the physical and mathematical model.  Analytical derivations are cross-checked (integration factor vs Laplace), and Python code is provided for numerical work.  While the literature scope could include more specialist numerical-analysis papers, the selected material is largely relevant and sufficient to sustain the investigation.  The evidence therefore aligns with the 7â9 band; a mid-band score of 8 reflects strong but not exhaustive research quality.

The discussion interprets results sensiblyâcontrasting the very large theoretical GTE bound with the tiny observed error, explaining the role of the Lipschitz constant and curvature, and suggesting the RungeâKutta method as a possible extension.  Strengths and limitations (single step size, neglect of round-off error) are acknowledged.  However, evaluation occasionally remains descriptive and would benefit from deeper quantitative comparison (e.g., testing multiple h values, benchmarking against higher-order methods).  The argument is reasoned and partially critical, placing it at the top of the 4â6 band; score 6.

The student employs a coherent and largely appropriate methodology: deriving the DE with KVL, solving analytically via Laplace transforms, implementing Eulerâs method in Python, and developing theoretical LTE/GTE bounds.  Sources are predominantly reliable textbooks and on-line references.  Evidence of informed choices is strong (e.g., abandoning integrating factor in favour of Laplace for efficiency, justifying the step size, linking theoretical error to practice).  However, the empirical component remains limited to one step size and one evaluation point, and no experimental or broader numerical validation is attempted.  This small limitation keeps the work just below the very top of the band, so a score of 5 is appropriate.

annotations.comment: The definition of the Laplace transform (eq. 4.2) is clearly stated and accurately formatted, supporting readersâ comprehension of the integral-transform methodology.
The proof of Lemma 4.2 is very detailed; consider relocating lengthy proofs to an appendix and summarizing key results in the main text to maintain narrative focus.
The two-stage integration by parts proof of the sine transform (eqs. 4.7â4.8) is logically sequenced and accurately applies advanced calculus concepts.
The title and research question are communicated clearly and prominently, immediately orienting the reader to the focus of the investigation and setting a precise purpose for the study.
Section headings and subheadings are clear and hierarchical, allowing the reader to navigate the prerequisite knowledge effectively.
Introduce the mathematical definition of current explicitly (i.e. i=âdq/dt) to reinforce the conceptual link between charge and current in the context of ODE modeling.
The word count is provided and is within the 4000-word limit, meeting IB requirements and demonstrating attention to formatting guidelines.
The table of contents is comprehensive and well organized, but the dot leaders vary in length; consider applying a consistent leader format to improve navigability.
The introduction is effectively structured with clear headings, guiding the reader logically from general context to specific research focus.
The application of KVL to derive the RL circuit ODE is logically presented, demonstrating clear understanding of circuit analysis principles.
When introducing the ODE (eq. 3.2 and 3.3), explicitly state all assumptions (zero initial current, constant component values) to strengthen methodological transparency.
Subject-specific terminology (ODE, series RL circuit, KVL) is used accurately, demonstrating solid understanding and application of electrical engineering concepts.
The lengthy Tinkercad diagram description disrupts the flow; consider moving detailed image descriptions to an appendix or caption to maintain narrative focus.
The citation of Tiliksew et al. lacks a date and full reference details; provide complete citation information to support academic rigor.
Equation 2.4 (KVL) is highlighted and positioned effectively, facilitating easy reference and reinforcing its foundational role in the analysis.
Figure numbering is inconsistent (skips from Figure 2 to Figure 4); renumber figures sequentially to maintain clarity and adhere to formatting conventions.
Substituting numerical component values to produce eq. 3.5 effectively transitions from general form to a concrete instance, enhancing clarity of the specific case studied.
The inductive proof of Lemma 4.2 demonstrates strong critical thinking by rigorously validating the transform of higher derivatives through a structured approach.
The statement of Lemma 4.3 interrupts the flow of the Laplace derivation; consider grouping all transform pair listings together for coherence before detailed proofs.
